note_data.title: Magnetic fields
keyPoints: Force fields are areas where objects experience non-contact forces, formed during interaction of masses, static charges or moving charges
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience a non-contact force, with uniform fields exerting the same force everywhere and radial fields where force depends on position
Gravitational field strength (g) is force per unit mass, calculated using g = F/m, constant in uniform fields but varies in radial fields
Newton's law of gravitation states gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
Gravitational force between point masses follows F = Gm₁m₂/r² where G is gravitational constant
For radial fields, gravitational field strength follows g = GM/r² showing an inverse square relationship
Mass of a uniform sphere acts as a point mass at its centre when calculating gravitational force on objects outside the sphere
Gravity always acts on objects with mass and is always attractive

definitions.definition: An area in which an object experiences a non-contact force
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object
A field that exerts the same gravitational force on a mass everywhere in the field
A field where the force exerted depends on the position of the object in the field

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a detailed account of the process or phenomenon step by step
Use given information to find a value, showing all working clearly

modelAnswer.question: A straight conductor of length 0.25 m carrying a current of 3.0 A is placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of flux density 0.40 T. Calculate the force on the conductor.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formula F = BIL or F = BIL sin θ
Mark 3: Correct final answer with unit

commonMistakes: Confusing gravitational field strength with gravitational force - field strength is force per unit mass
Forgetting that gravitational field strength varies with position in radial fields but is constant in uniform fields
Not recognizing that uniform sphere mass acts as point mass at centre for external calculations
Missing that gravitational force follows inverse square relationship with distance
examinerTips: Remember field line spacing indicates field strength - closer lines mean stronger gravitational field
Earth's gravitational field is radial but appears uniform very close to the surface
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Diagram showing a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field with arrows indicating: magnetic field direction (into/out of page using crosses/dots), current direction along conductor, and resultant force direction perpendicular to both using Fleming's left-hand rule
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe how to plot magnetic field lines around a bar magnet using iron filings and explain the direction of field lines
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question about the direction of magnetic field around a current-carrying wire, many students confused clockwise vs anticlockwise
M/J 2022 P2 Q6(b) (4 marks) — required explanation of how to increase strength of electromagnet, looking for number of turns, current, and core material
N/D 2023 P2 Q3(c) (3 marks) — asked to compare magnetic field strength at different distances from a magnet, testing inverse square relationship understanding

workedExample.question: A straight wire carries a current of 2.5 A. Describe the magnetic field produced around this wire and state how the direction of the field can be determined. Calculate the direction when current flows from left to right. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: State that the magnetic field forms concentric circles around the wire with the wire at the center
Step 2: Explain that field strength decreases with distance from the wire
Step 3: Apply right-hand grip rule - thumb points in direction of current (left to right), fingers curl in direction of magnetic field
Step 4: Therefore when current flows left to right, magnetic field circles clockwise when viewed from the left end
workedExample.answer: The magnetic field around a current-carrying wire forms concentric circles with the wire at the center. The field strength decreases with distance from the wire. The direction is found using the right-hand grip rule: thumb points along current direction, fingers curl showing field direction. With current flowing left to right, the magnetic field circles clockwise when viewed from the left.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for concentric circles, 1 mark for mentioning right-hand grip rule, 1 mark for correct application, 1 mark for stating clockwise direction

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused magnetic field lines with electric field lines' — always draw magnetic field lines as closed loops that never cross each other
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently drew field lines pointing in random directions' — remember magnetic field lines always go from North pole to South pole outside the magnet
Examiner Report 2023: 'Poor understanding of right-hand rules led to incorrect directions' — practice right-hand grip rule for wires and right-hand rule for motors until automatic
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw magnetic field lines around a bar magnet showing: curved lines emerging from North pole and entering South pole, lines never crossing, closer spacing near poles indicating stronger field, arrows on lines showing N→S direction, and field lines forming complete loops (though only external portion usually shown). For current-carrying wire: concentric circles around wire with arrows showing direction using right-hand grip rule.
note_data.quickRecap: Gravitational fields are regions where masses experience non-contact forces, with field strength representing force per unit mass. Newton's law shows gravitational force is proportional to mass product and inversely proportional to distance squared. Field strength in radial fields follows an inverse square relationship with distance from the source mass.
